summary: Asparouhova uses her decade-long experience as an ethical vegetarian to explore the impossibility of moral purity in technology infrastructure debates. She recounts how her strict vegetarianism gradually revealed unavoidable contradictions—from field mice killed in vegetable farming to animal products in tires and medication. She applies this lesson to current debates about whether infrastructure providers should de-platform bad actors, arguing that the logic quickly becomes untenable when extended to programming languages, email services, or phones. She concludes that while technology use is subject to moral debate, where we draw the line is a matter of policy rather than ethics, and we should approach these conversations carefully to avoid endless what-aboutism.
key_points: Strict ethical frameworks inevitably encounter edge cases that reveal their internal contradictions
The impulse to blame infrastructure providers after bad events stems from a desire to regain control and prevent future harm
Open source licenses deliberately avoid moral discrimination because attaching ethical instructions to technology is impossible
De-platforming logic, if extended consistently, leads to absurd conclusions about banning access to basic services
Where we draw the line on infrastructure responsibility is a policy question, not an ethical one
money_quotes: I realized that I was saying no not because they broke my moral framework, but because I wanted to uphold the idea of ethics-based vegetarianism.
I decided I wasn't comfortable ascribing to a theory that was impossible to fully practice.
Translating this theory to practice, however, is a Sisyphean endeavor.
There's a reason why open source licenses specify that the code 'must not discriminate against any person or group of persons'.
They recognized it's impossible to attach instructions to the invention and spread of technology, and they had the foresight to protect developers from liability.
Technology itself may be amoral, but what we do with technology is absolutely up for moral debate.
What starts out reasonably plausible will quickly devolve into a series of what-abouts.
key_insight: The impossibility of moral purity in infrastructure means that de-platforming decisions are ultimately policy choices rather than ethical imperatives.
